title: Coherent Dark State Formation of a Lead-Vacancy Spin Qubit in Diamond
authors: Yiyang Chen, Koyo Hirai, Tzyy Zheng Neo, Eiki Ota, Takashi Taniguchi, Masashi Miyakawa, Shinobu Onoda, Toshiharu Makino, Mutsuko Hatano, Takayuki Iwasaki
abstract: A lead-vacancy (PbV) center in diamond exhibits coherent emission above the liquid helium temperature, making it highly attractive for quantum network applications. Here, we report the magneto-optical and spin properties of PbV centers in diamond. We record a spin lifetime of 12 ms at 7.5 K under large off-axis magnetic field. Furthermore, we observe formation of the coherent dark state by coherent population trapping and estimate a spin dephasing time of 354 ns at 6.5 K. This work demonstrates the outstanding thermal robustness of the PbV spin compared to other group-IV centers above 4 K.
summary: This paper demonstrates that lead-vacancy centers in diamond can maintain long spin lifetimes and optical coherence at temperatures above 4 K. By achieving successful single-shot readout and observing coherent dark state formation, the study positions PbV centers as highly promising candidates for scalable, thermally robust quantum networks.
main_problem: The need for quantum emitters that can operate at higher temperatures (above 4 K) for scalable quantum networks while maintaining high optical coherence and suppressed spectral diffusion.
main_result: The PbV center in diamond demonstrates exceptional thermal robustness with a spin lifetime of 12 ms at 7.5 K and successful formation of a coherent dark state via coherent population trapping.
method: The study uses photoluminescence excitation (PLE) spectroscopy, all-optical spin initialization and single-shot readout (SSR), and coherent population trapping (CPT) to characterize spin properties.
model_or_system: A lead-vacancy (PbV) center in diamond, which is a spin-1/2 system with a large ground-state zero-field splitting of approximately 3900 GHz, implemented in a solid immersion lens (SIL) platform.
key_observables: Spin lifetime (T1), spin dephasing time (T2*), spin initialization fidelity, branching ratio, and saturation power.
important_parameters: Temperatures of 6.5 K to 13 K, magnetic fields up to 200 mT, spin lifetime of 12 ms at 7.5 K, and spin dephasing time of 354 ns at 6.5 K.
main_assumptions: The decoherence at 6.5 K is assumed to be limited by the spin bath rather than phonon-mediated processes.
figures_summary: Fig 1 shows the PbV energy level structure, PL spectrum, and PLE spectra under magnetic fields; Fig 2 details the spin initialization process, pulse sequences for SSR, and photon counting statistics.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the motivation for high-temperature emitters, describes the PbV system and experimental setup, presents spectroscopic characterization, details spin initialization and readout results, and concludes with the implications for quantum networking.
why_interesting: It demonstrates the use of coherent population trapping to create dark states in a solid-state system and provides experimental evidence of suppressed phonon-mediated decoherence due to large zero-field splitting, which is highly relevant to open quantum systems and quantum optics.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1: Magneto-optical properties of the PbV center. (a) Atomic structure and energy level of the PbV cen- ter in diamond. (b) Cryogenic photoluminescence (PL) spectrum under non-resonant 532 nm laser excitation. (c) PLE spectra under various magnetic fields at 13 K. The color corresponds to the magnetic field strength, varied from 0 to 200 mT in 20 mT increment. The solid lines represent fits using Lorentzian functions.

FIG. 2: Spin initialization and SSR performance of the PbV center at 7.5 K (a) Spin initialization. Inset shows a CFM image of the PbV in SIL. (b) Spin initialization rate as a function of the resonant laser power. Solid line denotes the fitting described in the main text. (c) Pulse sequence implemented for the SSR measurement. (d) Photon counting statistics derived from the sequence in (c). The threshold is set to one photon per pulse to discriminate the bright and dark states, yielding a SSR fidelity of 76%.
FIG. 3: Longitudinal relaxation time. (a) T1 measure- ment at 7.5 K. (b) Temperature dependence of T1.

FIG. 4: CPT of the PbV center. (a) Sequence and a representative CPT spectrum at 7 K. The black solid line represents a numerical fit based on the Lindblad master equation. (b) Evolution of the CPT spectra as varying the laser power at 6.5 K. (c) CPT linewidth as a function of the total incident laser power at 6.5 K. The narrowest observed linewidth is 0.9(6) MHz, corresponding to a spin dephasing time (T ∗ 2 ) of 354 ns.
